stories.title: Cosyland Children's Tower Stool Recall — 125,200 Units, Serious Injury Risk

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
RECALL: 125,200 Cosyland children's tower stools recalled by CPSC (May 14).

Stools can collapse, tip over, or trap a child's torso — 8 injuries reported.

Get a FREE repair kit at CPSC.gov or call 800-638-2772.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
Cosyland tower stools (models CS0003/CS0092-4) recalled by CPSC.

125,200 units. Collapse and entrapment risk. 8 injuries.

FREE repair kit: contact Cosyland Official or CPSC.gov / 800-638-2772.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Window: 8:00 AM MT

Colorado Springs parents — there is a safety recall that applies to a lot of families right now, and if you have a Cosyland children's tower stool, you need to act.

The Consumer Product Safety Commission announced on May 14 that 125,200 Cosyland tower stools (models CS0003 and CS0092-4) are recalled due to serious injury risks. The platform inside the stool can loosen over time, making the stool unstable. It can also collapse or tip over while in use — and the openings on the front and back are large enough for a child's torso to fit through, creating an entrapment hazard.

The CPSC has documented 25 reports of stability issues and falls, with eight injuries ranging from scrapes and bruises to a fractured arm.

These stools were sold on Amazon for about $70 from April 2021 through November 2025, in natural bamboo and gray finishes.

What to do: Stop using the stool immediately and contact Cosyland Official to receive a FREE repair kit, which includes protective nets, stabilizing feet, and installation instructions. The parts will be mailed to you directly.

You can also reach the CPSC at 800-638-2772 or visit CPSC.gov for more details.

Have you seen this recalled product in your home? Share this post so other COS families can check theirs.

---

article.headline: CPSC Recalls 125,200 Cosyland Children's Tower Stools — Collapse and Entrapment Risk

stories.title: D11 Graduation Week — Palmer High Today, Last Day of School Friday

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
Congratulations, Palmer High Class of 2026.

Ceremonies continue all week at Ed Robson Arena. Last day of school for D11 families: Friday, May 22.

Live streams available at d11.org.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
D11 graduation week is here. Ceremonies run May 19-21 at Ed Robson Arena, Colorado College.

Last day for most D11 students: Friday, May 22. Summer begins this weekend for Colorado Springs families.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Window: 10:00 AM MT

Colorado Springs, today marks the beginning of graduation week for District 11 families.

Palmer High School held its commencement ceremony this morning at 9 AM at Ed Robson Arena on the Colorado College campus. Ceremonies continue through Thursday, May 21, with additional graduations for Mitchell, Coronado, Doherty, and other D11 schools throughout the week.

The last day of school for most D11 students is this Friday, May 22 — which means Colorado Springs families are officially entering summer mode by the weekend.

If you cannot attend a ceremony in person, D11 has live streams available for all graduation ceremonies at d11.org.

To every graduate in the Class of 2026: this community is proud of you. To the families who got them there — you deserve recognition too.

What school are you celebrating this week?

---

article.headline: D11 Graduation Week Is Here — Palmer High Ceremony Today, Last Day of School Friday

stories.title: D11 Teacher Contract Dispute — 300+ Days In, What Parents Need to Know

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
D11 educators have been without a master contract for 300+ days (board voted 7-1 to sever it in Dec 2024).

Key issues: class sizes, counselors, special ed staffing.

Worth knowing as COS families plan for fall.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Window: 12:00 PM MT

As District 11 families wrap up the school year, there is a labor dispute in the background that is worth understanding — not just for this spring, but for what it may mean next fall.

In December 2024, the D11 School Board voted 7-1 to dissolve the 56-year-old master agreement with the Colorado Springs Education Association (CSEA), the teachers union. That decision triggered more than 300 days (and counting) without a formal contract governing the relationship between educators and the district.

In October 2025, about a third of D11 teachers participated in a one-day strike — the first in recent district history — after 91% of CSEA members voted in favor of strike action. Educators returned to work, but the contract dispute was not resolved.

CSEA says the lack of a contract has led to "decisions that are destabilizing schools, driving educators out of the profession, and shortchanging students." Their key concerns: rising class sizes, insufficient school counselors and social workers, underfunded special education support, and restrictions on curriculum content.

In early May 2026, educators and parents organized a community picket — more than 300 days after the contract was severed.

For Colorado Springs parents planning for the 2026-27 school year: this is the backdrop to watch. Class sizes, teacher staffing levels, and school support resources are all on the table.

What questions do you have about this situation? Drop them below.

---

article.headline: D11's Teacher Contract Dispute: What Colorado Springs Parents Need to Know

stories.title: YMCA Pikes Peak Region Summer Camps — Registration Open, Starts May 27

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
YMCA Pikes Peak Region summer camps start May 27 — registration is open now.

Three COS locations: Briargate, First & Main, Tri-Lakes. Themed weeks, sports, new Specialty Mini Camps.

Details: ppymca.org

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Window: 3:00 PM MT

With D11's last day of school on Friday, May 22, summer is officially here — and if your family still needs childcare or camp plans, the YMCA of the Pikes Peak Region has you covered.

Summer camps start May 27 across three Colorado Springs locations: Briargate, First & Main, and Tri-Lakes. Registration is open right now while spots last. If you plan to register, the Y asks that paperwork be completed at least three weeks before your child's start date.

Here is a quick look at what is planned:

**Week 1 (June 1-5):** "Vintage Colorado: No WiFi Edition" — a throwback to outdoor exploration, with activities like hiking and nature discovery.

**Week 2 (June 8-12):** "Low-Key Famous Artist Week" — creativity and art-focused programming.

**Throughout the summer:** Golf lessons, ninja warrior courses, rock climbing, and partnerships with local organizations.

**Sports Camps:** Tennis, pickleball, field games, and cheer camps for kids who want focused athletic development.

**New for 2026:** Morning Specialty Mini Camps — perfect for families who want engaging programming without a full-day commitment.

To learn more or register, visit ppymca.org. You can also contact individual locations directly: Briargate Y at 719.329.7298, First & Main Y at 719.884.8466, or Tri-Lakes Y at 719.481.8728.

What are your summer plans looking like, Colorado Springs?

---

article.headline: YMCA Pikes Peak Region Summer Camps Start May 27 — How to Register Before Spots Fill

stories.title: COS Summer Parks: What Families Need to Know Before Heading Out

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
COS summer parks update: ~25% of permanent restrooms are closed this season, and damaged playground equipment is being removed rather than repaired.

Check parks.coloradosprings.gov before you go.

#COSParenting #ColoradoSprings #COSKids #PikesPeakParenting

facebook.long_form:
Window: 5:00 PM MT

School ends Friday. Summer is here. Before you pack up the stroller and head to your neighborhood park, there are a few things Colorado Springs families should know about the 2026 park season.

The city announced earlier this year that budget cuts will affect parks this summer in two specific ways:

First, approximately 10 neighborhood parks (about 25% of permanent park restrooms) will have their restrooms closed for the 2026 season due to budget constraints. The city will maintain portable restroom availability in some areas, but not at the same coverage level as prior years.

Second, the city has scaled back its approach to playground maintenance. When playground equipment is damaged or vandalized, the current plan is to remove it rather than repair or replace it. Over time, this could affect the playground equipment available at some neighborhood parks.

On a more positive note for trail-loving families: the Rock Island Trail pedestrian bridge project is underway, with a 210-foot bridge being constructed over Sand Creek to connect the east and west sides of the trail. The project will eventually add more than two miles of new trail access in the city, funded by PPRTA II, CDOT grants, and the TOPS program. The project is expected to take 10-12 months.

Practical tips for families this summer: Before a big park trip, check the city parks website at parks.coloradosprings.gov for current facility status. For nearby restroom alternatives, many parks have portable units; also, nearby recreation centers, libraries, and businesses can be backups.

Where is your favorite park to take your family in COS this summer?

---

article.headline: COS Summer Parks 2026: What Families Need to Know Before You Head Out
